questions.question: Define e-waste and list four major sources of e-waste generation.
keyPoints: Definition: Discarded electrical/electronic devices at end of useful life
Sources: Household appliances (refrigerators, washing machines)
IT equipment (computers, printers, mobile phones)
Entertainment devices (TVs, music players)
Lighting equipment (LED, CFL bulbs)

questions.question: Explain the health impacts of lead and mercury exposure from e-waste.
keyPoints: Lead: Neurological damage, cognitive impairment in children, kidney damage, anemia, reproductive disorders
Mercury: Minamata disease, neurological damage, memory loss, tremors, vision impairment, birth defects
Both are persistent and bioaccumulate in body

questions.question: What is informal e-waste recycling? Describe two primitive techniques used and their hazards.
keyPoints: Definition: Untrained workers using unsafe methods
Technique 1: Open burning of cables to recover copper - releases toxic dioxins
Technique 2: Acid bath extraction of metals - releases toxic fumes, contaminates water
Hazards: Respiratory problems, skin diseases, environmental contamination

questions.question: List four toxic substances found in e-waste and their specific health effects.
keyPoints: 1. Lead - neurological damage, kidney problems
2. Mercury - Minamata disease, nervous system damage
3. Cadmium - kidney damage, bone fragility (Itai-itai)
4. Brominated flame retardants - endocrine disruption, cancer
5. Dioxins - cancer, immune system damage

questions.question: What is Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)? Explain its importance in e-waste management.
keyPoints: EPR makes manufacturers responsible for end-of-life collection/recycling
Producers must meet collection targets set by government
Finances recycling infrastructure through producer fees
Creates circular economy by encouraging design for recycling
Reduces burden on municipalities and informal sector

questions.question: Describe the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2016 and its key provisions.
keyPoints: Indian legislation governing e-waste handling
Key provisions: Producer responsibility to collect e-waste
Collection targets for manufacturers/importers
Must use authorized recyclers only
Restriction on hazardous substances
Annual reporting requirements
Penalties for non-compliance

questions.question: Discuss the environmental and health impacts of improper e-waste disposal. Suggest measures for sustainable e-waste management in India.
keyPoints: ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS: Soil contamination from heavy metals, Water pollution affecting aquatic life, Air pollution from burning, Bioaccumulation in food chain, Climate change from refrigerant gases
HEALTH IMPACTS: Respiratory diseases from inhalation, Neurological damage in children, Kidney and liver damage, Cancer risks, Birth defects

MEASURES: Strengthening E-waste Rules enforcement, Promoting formal recycling infrastructure, Public awareness campaigns, Extended Producer Responsibility implementation, Take-back programs by manufacturers, Developing regional collection centers, Training for informal sector workers, Public-private partnerships

questions.question: Explain the process of formal e-waste recycling. Include steps from collection to material recovery. Also discuss why informal recycling should be avoided.
keyPoints: FORMAL RECYCLING PROCESS: Collection → Transportation → Sorting → Dismantling → Shredding → Separation → Smelting/refining → Material recovery

WHY AVOID INFORMAL: No safety equipment for workers, Releases toxic substances without controls, Contaminates local environment, No proper disposal of hazardous waste, Exploitative labor practices, No value recovery from plastics/glass

questions.question: What is the Basel Convention? How does it address transboundary movement of e-waste? Discuss India's role in managing e-waste trade.
keyPoints: BASEL CONVENTION: International treaty (1989) administered by UNEP
PURPOSE: Controls transboundary movements of hazardous wastes
PRINCIPLE: Prior informed consent required before waste export
PROHIBITION: Cannot export hazardous waste from developed to developing countries without agreement

INDIA'S ROLE: Signed Basel Convention in 1992, Prohibited import of e-waste since 2000s, E-waste Rules 2016 restrict transboundary movement, Has become major e-waste generator requiring domestic management

questions.question: Describe the hierarchy of e-waste management. Also explain the concept of 'Urban Mining' and its benefits in context of resource recovery.
keyPoints: HIERARCHY: 1) Prevention - designing products to last longer, 2) Reuse - donating/selling functional used devices, 3) Recycling - processing to recover materials, 4) Energy recovery - incineration with energy capture, 5) Disposal - last resort in landfills
URBAN MINING: Recovery of valuable materials from e-waste instead of natural ores

BENEFITS: Lower energy consumption than mining, Reduces dependency on imports, Precious metals (gold, silver) concentration higher than ores, Reduces environmental impact of mining, Creates green jobs, Supports circular economy
